title: Docker COPY invalidates cache even when file contents haven't changed
body:
Docker's layer caching for COPY is based on file checksums, but it recalculates them every build. If your build context includes files that change frequently (like `.git` or logs), unrelated COPY instructions get invalidated.

```dockerfile
# Bad — entire context changing invalidates this
COPY . /app
RUN npm install

# Good — copy dependency files first, install, then copy source
COPY package.json package-lock.json /app/
RUN npm install
COPY . /app
```

Always structure your Dockerfile so that layers with the least frequent changes come first. Combined with a proper `.dockerignore`, this can reduce build times from minutes to seconds.

title: Docker multi-stage build ARGs don't persist across stages
body:
ARG values declared before the first FROM are global but must be re-declared (without a default) in each stage that needs them. ARG values declared inside a stage are scoped to that stage only.

```dockerfile
# Global ARG — available to FROM lines
ARG NODE_VERSION=20

FROM node:${NODE_VERSION}-alpine AS builder
# Must re-declare to use inside this stage
ARG NODE_VERSION
RUN echo "Building with Node ${NODE_VERSION}"

FROM node:${NODE_VERSION}-alpine AS runtime
# This ARG is NOT available here unless re-declared
ARG NODE_VERSION
RUN echo "Running with Node ${NODE_VERSION}"
```

Forgetting to re-declare the ARG inside a stage is a silent bug — the variable expands to an empty string with no error.

title: Docker ADD vs COPY — ADD has hidden auto-extraction behavior
body:
ADD automatically extracts tar archives and supports remote URLs. This sounds useful but creates unpredictable behavior and cache invalidation issues.

```dockerfile
# ADD extracts tar files automatically
ADD app.tar.gz /app/  # Extracted!
COPY app.tar.gz /app/  # Copied as-is

# ADD fetches remote URLs (but no caching headers)
ADD https://example.com/file.txt /app/  # Downloaded every build
```

Best practice: always use COPY unless you explicitly need tar extraction. For remote files, use `RUN curl` or `RUN wget` instead of ADD — you get better caching control and can verify checksums.

title: Docker HEALTHCHECK default interval is 30s with 30s start period
body:
If you add a HEALTHCHECK without specifying timing, Docker waits 0 seconds before the first check. Containers can be marked unhealthy before your app finishes starting.

```dockerfile
# Bad — first check runs immediately at container start
HEALTHCHECK CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1

# Good — give the app time to start
HEALTHCHECK --interval=15s --timeout=5s --start-period=30s --retries=3 \
  CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1
```

The `--start-period` is critical for apps with slow startup (JVM, large Node apps). During the start period, failed health checks don't count toward the retry limit. Without it, orchestrators like Swarm or ECS may restart containers in a crash loop.

title: Docker compose v2 uses dash instead of underscore for service names in networks
body:
Docker Compose v2 changed the default project separator from underscore to dash. This breaks DNS resolution if you hardcoded container hostnames.

```yaml
# compose.yaml
services:
  my-api:
    image: my-api:latest
    depends_on:
      - my-db
  my-db:
    image: postgres:16
```

In Compose v1, the container name was `myproject_my-db_1`. In v2, it's `myproject-my-db-1`. However, the DNS hostname for inter-service communication is just the service name (`my-db`), not the container name. If you were using container names for DNS resolution, switch to service names.

```python
# Correct — use the service name
DATABASE_URL = "postgresql://user:pass@my-db:5432/app"
# Wrong — don't use the container name
DATABASE_URL = "postgresql://user:pass@myproject-my-db-1:5432/app"
```

title: Docker volume mounts silently override container directory contents
body:
When you bind-mount a host directory to a container path, the host directory completely replaces whatever was in the container at that path — including files created during the build.

```yaml
# compose.yaml
services:
  app:
    build: .
    volumes:
      - .:/app  # Replaces everything in /app including node_modules!
```

```dockerfile
# Dockerfile
COPY package.json /app/
RUN npm install  # Creates /app/node_modules
COPY . /app
```

The `npm install` runs during build, but the bind mount overwrites `/app/node_modules` with whatever is (or isn't) on the host. Fix with a named volume for node_modules:

```yaml
volumes:
  - .:/app
  - /app/node_modules  # Anonymous volume preserves container's node_modules
```

title: Docker ENTRYPOINT exec form doesn't expand shell variables
body:
The exec form `["binary", "arg"]` does not invoke a shell, so environment variable expansion and globbing don't work.

```dockerfile
# This does NOT expand $PORT — runs literally "$PORT"
ENTRYPOINT ["node", "server.js", "--port", "$PORT"]

# Option 1: Use shell form (PID 1 issue though)
ENTRYPOINT node server.js --port $PORT

# Option 2: Use exec form with explicit shell
ENTRYPOINT ["/bin/sh", "-c", "node server.js --port $PORT"]

# Option 3 (best): Use an entrypoint script
COPY entrypoint.sh /entrypoint.sh
ENTRYPOINT ["/entrypoint.sh"]
```

Option 3 is preferred because the script can use `exec` to replace the shell process, ensuring proper signal handling:

```bash
#!/bin/sh
exec node server.js --port "${PORT:-3000}"
```

title: Docker signals not reaching app when using shell form CMD
body:
When you use shell form for CMD or ENTRYPOINT, your process runs as a child of `/bin/sh`, which doesn't forward signals. SIGTERM from `docker stop` never reaches your app, causing a 10-second timeout then SIGKILL.

```dockerfile
# Bad — node is PID 2, shell is PID 1, signals lost
CMD npm start

# Good — node is PID 1, receives signals directly
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

If you must use shell form (for variable expansion), use `exec`:

```dockerfile
CMD exec node server.js
```

Alternatively, use `tini` as an init system that properly forwards signals:

```dockerfile
RUN apk add --no-cache tini
ENTRYPOINT ["/sbin/tini", "--"]
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

title: Docker build context sent to daemon is larger than expected
body:
Docker sends the entire build context directory to the daemon before the build starts. Without a `.dockerignore`, this includes `.git`, `node_modules`, test fixtures, and other large directories.

```
# .dockerignore
.git
node_modules
*.md
.env*
dist
coverage
.next
__pycache__
*.pyc
.pytest_cache
.vscode
.idea
Dockerfile*
docker-compose*
```

You can verify what's being sent:

```bash
# Check context size
du -sh --exclude=.git . 

# Build with progress to see context transfer
docker build --progress=plain -t myapp .
# First line shows: "sending build context to docker daemon  XXX MB"
```

A 2GB context directory means Docker copies 2GB to the daemon on every build. A proper `.dockerignore` often reduces this to under 10MB.

title: Docker compose depends_on doesn't wait for service readiness
body:
`depends_on` only waits for the container to start, not for the service inside it to be ready. Your app will crash trying to connect to a database that hasn't finished initializing.

```yaml
services:
  app:
    build: .
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy  # Wait for health check
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
```

The `condition: service_healthy` syntax requires a healthcheck on the dependency. For services without health checks, implement retry logic in your application code:

```javascript
async function connectWithRetry(url, maxRetries = 10) {
  for (let i = 0; i < maxRetries; i++) {
    try { return await connect(url); }
    catch { await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 2000)); }
  }
  throw new Error('Failed to connect after retries');
}
```

title: Alpine-based images missing common libraries causing runtime crashes
body:
Alpine uses musl libc instead of glibc. Precompiled binaries (like Node native addons, Python wheels, or Go CGO binaries) built against glibc will segfault or fail to load on Alpine.

```dockerfile
# This can fail with "Error: Error loading shared library"
FROM node:20-alpine
RUN npm install bcrypt  # Native addon compiled against glibc

# Fix 1: Install compatibility packages
FROM node:20-alpine
RUN apk add --no-cache python3 make g++
RUN npm install bcrypt  # Recompiles from source

# Fix 2: Use slim instead of alpine
FROM node:20-slim
RUN npm install bcrypt  # Works out of the box
```

Alpine images are ~5MB smaller but the compatibility headaches often aren't worth it. `node:20-slim` (Debian-based) is ~75MB vs Alpine's ~50MB — a negligible difference for the reliability gain.

title: Docker buildx platform emulation is 10-50x slower than native builds
body:
Building ARM images on x86 (or vice versa) with `docker buildx` uses QEMU emulation, which is extremely slow — especially for compilation steps.

```bash
# Cross-platform build (slow — QEMU emulation)
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 -t myapp .

# Faster: use a remote native builder
docker buildx create --name remote-arm \
  --driver docker-container \
  --platform linux/arm64 \
  ssh://user@arm-server

docker buildx build --builder remote-arm \
  --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 -t myapp .
```

For CI, consider building each platform natively on its own runner and using `docker manifest` to combine them, rather than cross-compiling everything on one machine.

title: Docker container logs can fill up disk space with json-file driver
body:
The default `json-file` logging driver has no size limit. Long-running containers can generate gigabytes of logs that fill up the host disk.

```json
// /etc/docker/daemon.json — set global defaults
{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "10m",
    "max-file": "3"
  }
}
```

Per-container override in compose:

```yaml
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
    logging:
      driver: json-file
      options:
        max-size: "10m"
        max-file: "3"
```

To check current log sizes: `docker system df -v` or `du -sh /var/lib/docker/containers/*/`. To truncate a specific container's log: `truncate -s 0 $(docker inspect --format='{{.LogPath}}' container_name)`.

title: Docker USER directive doesn't create the user — you must create it first
body:
The USER instruction switches the user for subsequent instructions, but it doesn't create the user. If the user doesn't exist, the build fails or the container runs with a numeric UID that has no home directory.

```dockerfile
# Wrong — user doesn't exist
USER appuser
RUN npm install  # Fails: user not found

# Correct — create user first
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 appgroup && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 --ingroup appgroup appuser
USER appuser
WORKDIR /home/appuser/app
COPY --chown=appuser:appgroup . .
RUN npm install
```

Always pair USER with `--chown` on COPY/ADD instructions, otherwise files are owned by root and the non-root user can't read them.

title: Docker compose env_file doesn't support multiline values or quotes
body:
Docker Compose's `env_file` uses a simple parser that doesn't handle quoted strings the same way a shell does. Quotes become part of the value.

```bash
# .env file
DB_PASSWORD="my secret password"
# Actual value in container: "my secret password" (with quotes!)

# Correct for env_file:
DB_PASSWORD=my secret password
# Actual value: my secret password

# But this breaks if value has = sign:
CONNECTION_STRING=host=localhost port=5432
# Only gets: host
```

For complex values, use `environment` in your compose file directly, or use Docker secrets:

```yaml
services:
  app:
    environment:
      - DB_PASSWORD=my secret password
      - CONNECTION_STRING=host=localhost port=5432
```

title: Docker bridge network DNS resolution only works with user-defined networks
body:
Containers on the default `bridge` network cannot resolve each other by container name. Only user-defined bridge networks support automatic DNS.

```bash
# Default bridge — no DNS resolution
docker run --name db -d postgres
docker run --name app -d myapp
# Inside app: ping db → fails

# User-defined network — DNS works
docker network create mynet
docker run --name db --network mynet -d postgres
docker run --name app --network mynet -d myapp
# Inside app: ping db → resolves to db container IP
```

Docker Compose automatically creates a user-defined network, which is why service-to-service DNS works out of the box in Compose but not with raw `docker run`.

title: Docker named volumes persist data even after container removal
body:
Named volumes are not removed with `docker rm` or `docker compose down`. This is a feature, but it catches people off guard when they expect a fresh database after recreating containers.

```yaml
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
volumes:
  pgdata:
```

```bash
# This does NOT remove volumes
docker compose down

# This removes volumes too
docker compose down -v

# Check orphaned volumes
docker volume ls
docker volume prune  # Remove unused volumes
```

This is especially surprising when you change the database initialization scripts (like Postgres init SQL files) — the old volume still has data from the previous init, and the new scripts never run because the data directory isn't empty.

title: Docker EXPOSE doesn't actually expose ports — it's just documentation
body:
The EXPOSE instruction in a Dockerfile does nothing at runtime. It's metadata that documents which ports the container listens on, but doesn't publish them.

```dockerfile
# This does NOT make port 3000 accessible from the host
EXPOSE 3000
```

You still need `-p` to publish ports:

```bash
# Actually exposes the port
docker run -p 3000:3000 myapp

# EXPOSE + -P publishes all EXPOSE'd ports to random host ports
docker run -P myapp  # 3000 → 49153 (random)
```

The only functional use of EXPOSE is with `docker run -P` (capital P), which auto-maps all EXPOSE'd ports to random host ports. In Compose, use the `ports` directive — EXPOSE is irrelevant.

title: Docker RUN commands create a new layer — chain them to reduce image size
body:
Each RUN instruction creates a new filesystem layer. Files deleted in a later RUN still exist in the previous layer, inflating image size.

```dockerfile
# Bad — 3 layers, apt cache persists in layer 1
RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install -y curl
RUN rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Good — 1 layer, cache cleaned in same layer
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends curl && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
```

This is critical for package manager caches, build tools, and temporary files. If you install `gcc` to compile something, remove it in the same RUN or use multi-stage builds.

title: Docker compose profiles let you define optional services that don't start by default
body:
Services with profiles only start when explicitly requested. This is useful for dev tools, debug containers, and one-off tasks.

```yaml
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports: ["3000:3000"]
  db:
    image: postgres:16
  
  # Only starts with --profile debug
  pgadmin:
    image: dpage/pgadmin4
    profiles: ["debug"]
    ports: ["5050:80"]
  
  # Only starts with --profile seed
  seeder:
    build: ./seed
    profiles: ["seed"]
    depends_on: [db]
```

```bash
docker compose up           # Only app + db
docker compose --profile debug up  # app + db + pgadmin
docker compose --profile seed run seeder  # Run seeder one-off
```

Gotcha: a service with a profile that is a dependency of a non-profile service will NOT auto-start. You must explicitly activate the profile.

title: Docker resource limits are not enforced by default — containers can consume all host resources
body:
Without explicit limits, a container can use all available CPU and memory on the host, potentially crashing other containers or the host itself.

```yaml
# compose.yaml
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: '2.0'
          memory: 512M
        reservations:
          cpus: '0.5'
          memory: 256M
```

```bash
# docker run equivalent
docker run --cpus=2.0 --memory=512m --memory-swap=512m myapp
```

Important: `--memory` alone still allows swap usage. Set `--memory-swap` equal to `--memory` to disable swap. In Compose v2, resource limits under `deploy.resources` work even without Swarm mode.

title: Docker layer caching breaks when COPY includes frequently changing files
body:
Docker invalidates a COPY layer and all subsequent layers when any file in the copied set changes. A single changed file in a large directory busts the cache for everything after it.

```dockerfile
# Bad — any source change invalidates npm install cache
COPY . /app
RUN npm install
RUN npm run build

# Good — separate dependency install from source copy
COPY package.json package-lock.json /app/
RUN npm install --production
COPY . /app
RUN npm run build
```

For monorepos, copy workspace package.json files individually before the full COPY:

```dockerfile
COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml pnpm-workspace.yaml /app/
COPY packages/api/package.json /app/packages/api/
COPY packages/web/package.json /app/packages/web/
RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
COPY . /app
```

title: Docker compose watch restarting on every file save in large projects
body:
Compose Watch (the replacement for bind mounts in dev) can trigger excessive rebuilds if the watch path is too broad.

```yaml
services:
  app:
    build: .
    develop:
      watch:
        # Sync source files without rebuild
        - action: sync
          path: ./src
          target: /app/src
        # Rebuild only when dependencies change
        - action: rebuild
          path: ./package.json
        # Sync+restart when config changes
        - action: sync+restart
          path: ./config
          target: /app/config
```

```bash
docker compose watch  # Starts watch mode
```

Gotcha: don't set `action: rebuild` on a broad path like `./src` — use `sync` for source files and reserve `rebuild` for dependency changes. The `sync` action is like a live bind mount but works with remote Docker hosts too.

title: Docker secrets in Swarm are mounted as files, not environment variables
body:
Docker Swarm secrets are mounted at `/run/secrets/<name>` as files. Libraries that read from environment variables won't find them automatically.

```yaml
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
    secrets:
      - db_password
    environment:
      # Read from the secret file
      DB_PASSWORD_FILE: /run/secrets/db_password
secrets:
  db_password:
    file: ./secrets/db_password.txt
```

Your app must read the file:

```javascript
const fs = require('fs');
const dbPassword = process.env.DB_PASSWORD_FILE
  ? fs.readFileSync(process.env.DB_PASSWORD_FILE, 'utf8').trim()
  : process.env.DB_PASSWORD;
```

Many official images (Postgres, MySQL, Redis) support the `_FILE` suffix convention natively, e.g., `POSTGRES_PASSWORD_FILE`.

title: Docker WORKDIR creates the directory if it doesn't exist
body:
WORKDIR implicitly creates the directory and all parent directories. This can mask issues where you intended to COPY into an existing path.

```dockerfile
# WORKDIR creates /app/src even if it doesn't exist
WORKDIR /app/src
RUN ls  # Empty directory, no error

# Common mistake: typo in WORKDIR path
WORKDIR /ap/src  # Creates /ap/src — no error!
COPY . .  # Files end up in wrong place
```

WORKDIR also affects all subsequent RUN, CMD, ENTRYPOINT, COPY, and ADD instructions. Always use absolute paths with WORKDIR to avoid confusion about the current directory.

title: Docker compose extends is back in Compose v2 but with limitations
body:
Compose v2 re-introduced `extends` (it was removed in Compose v3 for Swarm). But extended services cannot have `depends_on`, `links`, or `volumes_from` — these fields are not inherited.

```yaml
# compose.base.yaml
services:
  base-node:
    image: node:20-slim
    working_dir: /app
    environment:
      NODE_ENV: production

# compose.yaml
services:
  api:
    extends:
      file: compose.base.yaml
      service: base-node
    ports: ["3000:3000"]
    command: node api.js
  worker:
    extends:
      file: compose.base.yaml
      service: base-node
    command: node worker.js
```

If you need to share `depends_on`, use YAML anchors instead of `extends`.

title: Docker image scanning misses vulnerabilities in non-package files
body:
Docker security scanners (Trivy, Snyk, Scout) only detect vulnerabilities in known package managers (apt, apk, npm, pip). Binaries copied directly or compiled from source are invisible to scanners.

```bash
# Scan with Docker Scout
docker scout quickview myapp:latest
docker scout cves myapp:latest

# Scan with Trivy
trivy image myapp:latest

# Scan with Snyk
snyk container test myapp:latest
```

Gotcha: if you `COPY` a precompiled binary or download a tarball with `curl`, no scanner will flag vulnerabilities in it. Always prefer installing via package managers when possible, and pin versions explicitly:

```dockerfile
# Scannable — version tracked by package manager
RUN apk add --no-cache curl=8.5.0-r0

# Not scannable — invisible to security tools
RUN curl -L https://example.com/tool-v1.2.3 -o /usr/local/bin/tool
```

title: Docker build cache is not shared between BuildKit and legacy builder
body:
Docker Desktop uses BuildKit by default, but CI systems or older Docker versions may use the legacy builder. The two have separate caches and different cache behaviors.

```bash
# Check which builder is active
DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build .  # Force BuildKit
DOCKER_BUILDKIT=0 docker build .  # Force legacy builder

# BuildKit supports inline cache for registry-based caching
docker build --build-arg BUILDKIT_INLINE_CACHE=1 -t myapp .
docker push myapp:latest

# Pull cache from registry on CI
docker build --cache-from myapp:latest -t myapp .
```

BuildKit also supports cache mounts that persist across builds:

```dockerfile
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.npm npm install
```

This keeps the npm cache between builds without including it in the final image layer.

title: Docker container timezone defaults to UTC regardless of host timezone
body:
Containers use UTC by default. Applications logging timestamps or scheduling tasks will use UTC unless explicitly configured.

```dockerfile
# Set timezone in Dockerfile (Debian/Ubuntu)
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends tzdata && \
    ln -sf /usr/share/zoneinfo/America/New_York /etc/localtime && \
    echo "America/New_York" > /etc/timezone

# Alpine
RUN apk add --no-cache tzdata && \
    cp /usr/share/zoneinfo/America/New_York /etc/localtime && \
    echo "America/New_York" > /etc/timezone
```

Or pass via environment:

```yaml
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
    environment:
      - TZ=America/New_York
```

Best practice: keep containers in UTC and handle timezone conversion at the application level. This avoids confusion when containers run across different regions.

title: Docker rootless mode has limited network and storage driver support
body:
Rootless Docker runs the daemon as a non-root user for security, but comes with limitations: no binding to ports below 1024, limited storage drivers, and no `--privileged` flag.

```bash
# Install rootless Docker
dockerctl-rootless.sh install

# Set environment
export DOCKER_HOST=unix://$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/docker.sock

# Port mapping below 1024 requires net.ipv4.ip_unprivileged_port_start
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_unprivileged_port_start=80
```

Rootless Docker uses `fuse-overlayfs` instead of the native overlay2 driver, which can be slower. For production, Podman (daemonless) is often a better choice for rootless container workflows since it was designed rootless-first.

title: Docker error: no space left on device during build
body:
This error occurs when Docker's storage is full — not necessarily the host disk. Docker stores images, containers, volumes, and build cache separately.

```bash
# Check Docker disk usage
docker system df

# Clean up everything unused
docker system prune -a --volumes

# More targeted cleanup
docker image prune -a        # Remove all unused images
docker container prune        # Remove stopped containers
docker volume prune           # Remove unused volumes
docker builder prune          # Remove build cache

# Nuclear option — remove ALL Docker data
docker system prune -a --volumes -f
```

In CI, add `docker system prune -f` at the start of each pipeline. For Docker Desktop, increase the disk image size in Settings > Resources > Advanced.

title: Docker error: bind mount permission denied on Linux host
body:
When running a container as a non-root user, bind-mounted files may be inaccessible because the container user's UID doesn't match the host file owner.

```bash
# Error: EACCES: permission denied, open '/app/data/file.txt'

# Fix 1: Match UIDs
docker run -u $(id -u):$(id -g) -v ./data:/app/data myapp

# Fix 2: Set permissions in Dockerfile
RUN chown -R 1000:1000 /app/data

# Fix 3: Use user namespace remapping
# /etc/docker/daemon.json
{
  "userns-remap": "default"
}
```

On Docker Desktop for Mac/Windows, this isn't an issue because file sharing uses a virtualization layer that maps permissions automatically. It's a Linux-only problem.

title: Docker error: Cannot connect to the Docker daemon
body:
The most common Docker error. It means the Docker daemon isn't running or your user doesn't have permission to access the socket.

```bash
# Check if daemon is running
systemctl status docker
sudo systemctl start docker

# Check socket permissions
ls -la /var/run/docker.sock

# Add user to docker group (Linux)
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
newgrp docker  # Apply without logout

# macOS: start Docker Desktop
open -a Docker

# If using a custom socket
export DOCKER_HOST=unix:///var/run/docker.sock
```

In CI environments (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI), ensure the Docker service is started in the pipeline config. For Docker-in-Docker (DinD), mount the host socket or use the `docker:dind` service.

title: Docker error: port is already allocated
body:
This error occurs when the host port is already in use by another container or process.

```bash
# Find what's using the port
lsof -i :3000
netstat -tlnp | grep 3000

# Find which container uses the port
docker ps --format '{{.Names}}: {{.Ports}}' | grep 3000

# Fix: use a different host port
docker run -p 3001:3000 myapp

# Or stop the conflicting container
docker stop $(docker ps -q --filter publish=3000)
```

In Compose, if you have multiple compose files running, they may conflict on ports. Use `docker compose ps` to see all running services and their port mappings.

Pro tip: use port 0 to let Docker assign a random available port:
```bash
docker run -p 0:3000 myapp
docker port <container> 3000  # Shows assigned port
```

title: Docker error: OCI runtime create failed — exec format error
body:
This happens when you try to run an image built for a different CPU architecture (e.g., running an ARM image on x86 or vice versa).

```bash
# Check image architecture
docker inspect myapp --format '{{.Architecture}}'

# Check host architecture
uname -m

# Pull the correct platform
docker pull --platform linux/amd64 myapp:latest

# Build for the correct platform
docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t myapp .

# Enable multi-platform support via QEMU
docker run --privileged --rm tonistiigi/binfmt --install all
```

This is increasingly common with Apple Silicon Macs. Always specify `--platform linux/amd64` in CI/CD pipelines to ensure consistent builds across developer machines and servers.

title: Docker error: max depth exceeded when using too many layers
body:
Docker images have a layer limit (historically 127 layers with AUFS, higher with overlay2). Deep multi-stage builds or images based on many FROM layers can hit this.

```dockerfile
# Each instruction adds a layer
RUN command1  # Layer
RUN command2  # Layer
RUN command3  # Layer

# Fix: combine RUN instructions
RUN command1 && \
    command2 && \
    command3
```

With overlay2 (the modern default), the limit is much higher but the real issue is performance — hundreds of layers slow down image pulls and container starts. Keep total layers under 20 for optimal performance by combining related RUN instructions.

title: Docker error: network has active endpoints when removing network
body:
You can't remove a Docker network while containers are still connected to it.

```bash
# Error: network mynet has active endpoints

# Find connected containers
docker network inspect mynet --format '{{range .Containers}}{{.Name}} {{end}}'

# Disconnect all containers
docker network disconnect -f mynet container_name

# Or stop all containers using the network
docker ps --filter network=mynet -q | xargs docker stop

# Now remove the network
docker network rm mynet

# Nuclear: remove all unused networks
docker network prune
```

In Compose, `docker compose down` handles this automatically. The error usually happens when you manually created a network and forgot to clean up containers.

title: Docker error: COPY failed file not found — build context confusion
body:
COPY paths are relative to the build context (the directory passed to `docker build`), not the Dockerfile location.

```bash
# Build context is current directory
docker build -t myapp .

# Build context is parent directory
docker build -t myapp -f docker/Dockerfile ..
```

```dockerfile
# If Dockerfile is in docker/ but build context is project root:
COPY src/ /app/src/           # Correct — relative to build context (project root)
COPY docker/nginx.conf /etc/  # Correct
COPY ../shared/ /app/shared/  # ERROR — can't go above build context
```

You cannot COPY files from outside the build context. The build context defines the boundary. If you need files from a parent directory, set the build context to the parent and use `-f` to specify the Dockerfile location.

title: Docker error: container unhealthy after healthcheck fails
body:
When a container's health check fails `retries` times in a row, Docker marks it as unhealthy. This can trigger restarts in orchestrators.

```bash
# Check health status
docker inspect --format='{{json .State.Health}}' mycontainer | jq

# See health check logs
docker inspect --format='{{range .State.Health.Log}}{{.Output}}{{end}}' mycontainer

# Common issues:
# 1. Health check command not installed in image
# Fix: install curl/wget in Dockerfile
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl

# 2. Wrong port or path
HEALTHCHECK CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/health  # Is it really 3000?

# 3. App hasn't started yet — increase start-period
HEALTHCHECK --start-period=60s CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/health
```

To disable a health check inherited from a base image: `HEALTHCHECK NONE`.

title: Docker error: image with reference was not found — tag was overwritten
body:
If you pull `myapp:latest` and someone pushes a new version with the same tag, your local image becomes untagged (`<none>:<none>`) and any reference to the tag pulls the new version.

```bash
# These images are dangling — old tags overwritten
docker images --filter 'dangling=true'

# Pin versions instead of using :latest
docker pull myapp:1.2.3

# Even better — use digest for immutable references
docker pull myapp@sha256:abc123...

# In Compose, pin with digest
services:
  app:
    image: myapp@sha256:abc123def456...
```

In production, never deploy `:latest`. Use semantic versioning or commit SHAs as tags. In CI, output the full image digest and use it in deployment manifests.

title: Docker error: container exits immediately with code 0
body:
A container exits when its main process (PID 1) exits. If your CMD runs a background process, the container stops immediately because the foreground shell exits.

```dockerfile
# Bad — starts nginx in background, shell exits, container stops
CMD nginx

# Good — run in foreground
CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]

# Bad — runs process in background
CMD node server.js &

# Good — run in foreground
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

For debugging, run the container interactively:

```bash
docker run -it myapp /bin/sh
# Then manually run your CMD to see what happens
```

Another common cause: the entrypoint script doesn't use `exec`, so when the script finishes, PID 1 (the shell) exits even though the app is still running as a child process.

title: Docker error: toomanyrequests — Docker Hub rate limiting
body:
Docker Hub limits anonymous pulls to 100 per 6 hours, and authenticated free accounts to 200 per 6 hours. CI pipelines frequently hit this.

```bash
# Check remaining pulls
TOKEN=$(curl -s "https://auth.docker.io/token?service=registry.docker.io&scope=repository:library/alpine:pull" | jq -r .token)
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://registry-1.docker.io/v2/ratelimitpreview/test/manifests/latest" -I 2>&1 | grep ratelimit

# Fix 1: Login to Docker Hub
docker login -u username

# Fix 2: Use a mirror/proxy registry
# /etc/docker/daemon.json
{
  "registry-mirrors": ["https://mirror.gcr.io"]
}

# Fix 3: Copy base images to your own registry
docker pull node:20-slim
docker tag node:20-slim ghcr.io/myorg/node:20-slim
docker push ghcr.io/myorg/node:20-slim
```

In CI, always cache pulled images and authenticate with Docker Hub.

title: Docker error: dns resolution failed in container during build
body:
RUN instructions that fetch from the internet (apt-get, npm install, pip install) fail with DNS errors when Docker's DNS configuration doesn't match the host.

```bash
# Error: Could not resolve host: registry.npmjs.org

# Fix 1: Use host DNS in build
docker build --network=host -t myapp .

# Fix 2: Configure Docker DNS
# /etc/docker/daemon.json
{
  "dns": ["8.8.8.8", "8.8.4.4"]
}
sudo systemctl restart docker

# Fix 3: Compose build with network
services:
  app:
    build:
      context: .
      network: host
```

This is especially common in corporate environments with custom DNS servers, VPNs, or proxy configurations. Docker Desktop for Mac has its own DNS resolution that sometimes conflicts with VPN software.

title: Docker error: failed to compute cache key — file not found in context
body:
BuildKit's error message when COPY can't find a file. Different from the legacy builder's error — check your `.dockerignore` first.

```bash
# Error: failed to compute cache key: "/src" not found

# Common cause: file is in .dockerignore
cat .dockerignore
# If it shows 'src/' — that's your problem

# Another cause: build context is wrong
docker build -f Dockerfile .  # Context is .
docker build -f Dockerfile ..  # Context is parent dir

# Debug: list files in build context
# (BuildKit doesn't show context transfer progress by default)
DOCKER_BUILDKIT=0 docker build .  # Legacy builder shows context size
```

BuildKit only transfers files that are actually referenced by COPY/ADD instructions (lazy context), so the error means the file truly isn't in the context or is excluded by `.dockerignore`.

title: Docker error: insufficient memory — container killed by OOM
body:
When a container exceeds its memory limit, the kernel's OOM killer terminates it with exit code 137 (128 + SIGKILL signal 9).

```bash
# Check if container was OOM killed
docker inspect mycontainer --format='{{.State.OOMKilled}}'

# Check container memory usage
docker stats mycontainer --no-stream

# Increase memory limit
docker run --memory=1g --memory-swap=1g myapp

# In Compose
services:
  app:
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 1G
```

For Node.js, also set the V8 heap limit:
```dockerfile
CMD ["node", "--max-old-space-size=768", "server.js"]
```

The V8 heap limit should be ~75% of the container memory limit to leave room for other memory usage (buffers, native addons, etc.).

title: Docker error: context canceled during multi-stage build
body:
BuildKit parallelizes multi-stage builds. If one stage fails, it cancels all other running stages, producing a confusing "context canceled" error on the stages that were actually succeeding.

```
[builder 3/5] RUN npm install
 => ERROR: context canceled
[test 2/3] RUN npm test
 => ERROR: npm ERR! Test failed  <-- actual error
```

The real error is in the stage that failed first, not the "context canceled" ones. Read BuildKit output from bottom to top or search for the first ERROR that isn't "context canceled".

To debug, build a specific stage in isolation:

```bash
# Build only the failing stage
docker build --target test -t myapp-test .
```

title: Docker error: /bin/sh not found when using distroless base image
body:
Distroless images (like `gcr.io/distroless/static` or `gcr.io/distroless/base`) don't have a shell. Shell-form CMD/RUN fails, and you can't exec into the container.

```dockerfile
# Fails — no /bin/sh in distroless
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static
CMD node server.js  # Shell form requires /bin/sh

# Works — exec form doesn't need shell
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static
CMD ["/app/server"]  # Direct execution

# Use debug variant for troubleshooting
FROM gcr.io/distroless/base:debug
# Now you can: docker exec -it container /busybox/sh
```

Distroless images are great for security (minimal attack surface) but require all RUN commands in a builder stage. The final stage should only COPY binaries and use exec-form CMD.

title: Docker error: compose file version is obsolete — remove version field
body:
Docker Compose v2 ignores the `version` field. Including it produces a warning, and old version-specific features may not work as expected.

```yaml
# Old — produces warning
version: '3.8'
services:
  app:
    image: myapp

# Correct for Compose v2 — no version field
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
```

Compose v2 merged all features from all version specifications. Features that were `deploy`-only in v3 (like `resources`, `restart_policy`) now work without Swarm. If migrating from v2 or v3 files, just remove the `version` line and test.

title: Docker error: cannot mount volume over existing directory with contents
body:
When using named volumes, Docker copies the container directory's contents into the volume on first use. But bind mounts always overlay — overwriting what's in the container.

```yaml
# Named volume — container contents are copied to volume (first time only)
volumes:
  - mydata:/app/data  # /app/data contents preserved

# Bind mount — host directory overlays container contents
volumes:
  - ./data:/app/data  # Container's /app/data is hidden
```

This difference catches people when switching from named volumes to bind mounts during development. If you need the container's files, initialize the bind mount directory first:

```bash
# Copy files from container to host
docker create --name temp myapp
docker cp temp:/app/data ./data
docker rm temp
```

title: Multi-stage Docker build for Node.js production image
body:
A production-optimized multi-stage build that separates build dependencies from runtime, resulting in a minimal final image.

```dockerfile
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1

# Stage 1: Install dependencies
FROM node:20-slim AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --ignore-scripts

# Stage 2: Build
FROM deps AS builder
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 3: Production dependencies only
FROM node:20-slim AS prod-deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev --ignore-scripts

# Stage 4: Runtime
FROM node:20-slim AS runtime
RUN addgroup --system app && adduser --system --ingroup app app
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=prod-deps --chown=app:app /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=builder --chown=app:app /app/dist ./dist
COPY --chown=app:app package.json ./
USER app
EXPOSE 3000
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --start-period=10s \
  CMD node -e "require('http').get('http://localhost:3000/health', r => r.statusCode === 200 ? process.exit(0) : process.exit(1))"
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

This pattern yields images ~150MB instead of ~1GB. The health check uses Node directly instead of curl (no need to install additional tools).

title: Docker Compose development environment with hot reload and debug ports
body:
A complete development Compose setup with hot reload, debugging, and local services.

```yaml
# compose.yaml
services:
  app:
    build:
      context: .
      target: development  # Multi-stage target
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
      - "9229:9229"  # Node.js debugger
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=development
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:postgres@db:5432/myapp
      - REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379
    develop:
      watch:
        - action: sync
          path: ./src
          target: /app/src
        - action: rebuild
          path: package.json
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_started

  db:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp
      POSTGRES_USER: postgres
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./init.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"

volumes:
  pgdata:
```

Run with `docker compose watch` for automatic sync on file changes. Attach VS Code debugger to port 9229 for breakpoint debugging.

title: Docker health check patterns for different application types
body:
Health checks should verify that the application is actually serving requests, not just that the process is running.

```dockerfile
# HTTP server (no curl needed)
HEALTHCHECK --interval=15s --timeout=5s --start-period=20s --retries=3 \
  CMD node -e "require('http').get('http://localhost:3000/health', r => { process.exit(r.statusCode === 200 ? 0 : 1) }).on('error', () => process.exit(1))"

# Python HTTP server
HEALTHCHECK CMD python -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlopen('http://localhost:8000/health')"

# PostgreSQL
HEALTHCHECK CMD pg_isready -U postgres -d mydb

# Redis
HEALTHCHECK CMD redis-cli ping | grep PONG

# Worker process (check PID file or recent output)
HEALTHCHECK CMD test -f /tmp/worker.pid && kill -0 $(cat /tmp/worker.pid)

# gRPC service
HEALTHCHECK CMD grpc_health_probe -addr=:50051
```

For Compose orchestration, health checks control `depends_on` ordering with `condition: service_healthy`. Keep health check endpoints lightweight — no database queries or external API calls.

title: Docker BuildKit cache mounts for faster dependency installation
body:
Cache mounts persist a directory across builds without including it in the image. This dramatically speeds up package manager operations.

```dockerfile
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1

# Node.js — cache npm/yarn/pnpm
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.npm \
    npm ci

RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.yarn/berry/cache \
    yarn install --immutable

RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.local/share/pnpm/store \
    pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

# Python — cache pip
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/pip \
    pip install -r requirements.txt

# Go — cache module downloads and build cache
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/go/pkg/mod \
    --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/go-build \
    go build -o /app/server .

# Rust — cache cargo registry and build artifacts
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/usr/local/cargo/registry \
    --mount=type=cache,target=/app/target \
    cargo build --release

# apt — cache downloaded packages
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/var/cache/apt \
    --mount=type=cache,target=/var/lib/apt/lists \
    apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl
```

Cache mounts require BuildKit (`DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1` or Docker Desktop). The cache persists on the builder, not in the image — perfect for CI when using persistent runners.

title: Docker networking pattern — isolating frontend and backend networks
body:
Use multiple networks to control which services can communicate. The frontend proxy shouldn't have direct database access.

```yaml
services:
  nginx:
    image: nginx
    ports: ["80:80"]
    networks: [frontend]
    depends_on: [api]

  api:
    build: .
    networks: [frontend, backend]  # Bridge between networks
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:secret@db:5432/app

  db:
    image: postgres:16
    networks: [backend]  # Only accessible from backend network
    volumes: [pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data]

  redis:
    image: redis:7
    networks: [backend]

networks:
  frontend:
  backend:

volumes:
  pgdata:
```

With this setup, nginx can reach api but NOT db or redis directly. The api service is on both networks, acting as a bridge. This follows the principle of least privilege for container networking.

title: Docker Compose override files for environment-specific config
body:
Use override files to layer environment-specific configuration on top of a base compose file.

```yaml
# compose.yaml (base)
services:
  app:
    image: myapp:latest
    environment:
      NODE_ENV: production
    restart: always

  db:
    image: postgres:16
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  pgdata:
```

```yaml
# compose.override.yaml (auto-loaded in dev)
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports: ["3000:3000", "9229:9229"]
    environment:
      NODE_ENV: development
    volumes:
      - .:/app
    restart: "no"

  db:
    ports: ["5432:5432"]
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: devpass
```

```yaml
# compose.prod.yaml (explicit for prod)
services:
  app:
    deploy:
      replicas: 3
      resources:
        limits: { memory: 512M }
  db:
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD_FILE: /run/secrets/db_password
```

```bash
# Dev: auto-uses compose.yaml + compose.override.yaml
docker compose up

# Prod: explicit file selection
docker compose -f compose.yaml -f compose.prod.yaml up -d
```

title: Docker graceful shutdown pattern with proper signal handling
Docker CI/CD pipeline with registry caching and multi-platform builds
body:
An optimized Docker build pipeline for GitHub Actions using registry cache and multi-platform support.

```yaml
# .github/workflows/docker.yml
name: Build and Push
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    tags: ['v*']

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3
      - uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        id: meta
        with:
          images: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}
          tags: |
            type=ref,event=branch
            type=semver,pattern={{version}}
            type=sha,prefix=

      - uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: .
          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max
```

GitHub Actions cache (`type=gha`) is faster than registry cache for GHA runners. `mode=max` caches all layers, not just the final stage.

title: Docker Compose one-off task pattern for migrations and seeds
body:
Use Compose for one-off tasks like database migrations, seeding, and maintenance scripts without keeping long-running services.

```yaml
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready"]
      interval: 5s
      retries: 5

  migrate:
    build: .
    profiles: ["tools"]  # Don't start with `up`
    command: npx prisma migrate deploy
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@db:5432/myapp

  seed:
    build: .
    profiles: ["tools"]
    command: node scripts/seed.js
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
```

```bash
# Run migration (starts db, waits for healthy, runs migrate, exits)
docker compose run --rm migrate

# Run seed
docker compose run --rm seed

# Or use --profile for multiple tools
docker compose --profile tools run --rm migrate
```

The `--rm` flag removes the container after exit. Using `profiles: ["tools"]` ensures these services don't start with `docker compose up`.

title: Docker security hardening — read-only filesystem and dropped capabilities
body:
Harden containers by restricting filesystem access and Linux capabilities.

```yaml
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
    read_only: true  # Immutable root filesystem
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp  # Writable temp directory
      - /var/run  # For PID files
    security_opt:
      - no-new-privileges:true  # Prevent privilege escalation
    cap_drop:
      - ALL  # Drop all Linux capabilities
    cap_add:
      - NET_BIND_SERVICE  # Only add what's needed
```

```bash
# docker run equivalent
docker run --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp \
  --security-opt no-new-privileges:true \
  --cap-drop ALL \
  --cap-add NET_BIND_SERVICE \
  myapp
```

Combine with a non-root USER, a minimal base image (distroless or Alpine), and no setuid binaries for defense in depth. Run `docker run --rm -it myapp /bin/sh -c 'find / -perm -4000'` to find setuid binaries in your image.

title: Docker log aggregation pattern with structured JSON logging
body:
Configure containers to output structured JSON logs for easy parsing by log aggregation tools.

```javascript
// Application code — output JSON logs to stdout
const log = (level, message, meta = {}) => {
  console.log(JSON.stringify({
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
    level,
    message,
    service: process.env.SERVICE_NAME || 'api',
    ...meta
  }));
};

log('info', 'Request handled', { method: 'GET', path: '/api/users', duration: 45 });
```

```yaml
# compose.yaml with fluentd logging driver
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
    logging:
      driver: fluentd
      options:
        fluentd-address: localhost:24224
        tag: myapp.{{.Name}}

  # Or use json-file with labels for Docker log queries
  api:
    image: myapi
    logging:
      driver: json-file
      options:
        max-size: "10m"
        max-file: "3"
        labels: "service,environment"
    labels:
      service: api
      environment: production
```

Always log to stdout/stderr, never to files inside the container. Let Docker's logging driver handle rotation and forwarding.

title: Docker init containers pattern using Compose for setup tasks
body:
Kubernetes has init containers natively. In Docker Compose, you can simulate the pattern using depends_on with service_completed_successfully.

```yaml
services:
  # Init container: run migrations before app starts
  init-db:
    image: myapp
    command: npx prisma migrate deploy
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    restart: "no"  # Run once only

  # Init container: wait for external service
  init-check-api:
    image: curlimages/curl
    command: sh -c 'until curl -sf http://external-api:8080/health; do sleep 2; done'
    restart: "no"

  app:
    build: .
    depends_on:
      init-db:
        condition: service_completed_successfully
      init-check-api:
        condition: service_completed_successfully
      db:
        condition: service_healthy

  db:
    image: postgres:16
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready"]
      interval: 5s
      retries: 5
```

The `service_completed_successfully` condition ensures the init containers ran and exited with code 0 before the app starts.

title: Docker build secrets for private registries and API keys during build
body:
BuildKit secrets let you use sensitive data during the build without embedding it in the image layers.

```dockerfile
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1

FROM node:20-slim
WORKDIR /app

# Mount npm token as secret during install
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=npmrc,target=/root/.npmrc \
    npm ci

# Mount SSH key for private git repos
RUN --mount=type=ssh \
    git clone git@github.com:private/repo.git

# Mount arbitrary secret files
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=api_key \
    API_KEY=$(cat /run/secrets/api_key) && \
    curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_KEY" https://api.example.com/data > data.json
```

```bash
# Pass secrets at build time
docker build --secret id=npmrc,src=$HOME/.npmrc \
             --secret id=api_key,src=./api_key.txt \
             --ssh default \
             -t myapp .
```

Secrets are never stored in the image layers, even if the build fails midway. This replaces the insecure pattern of using ARG for secrets.

title: Docker Compose variable interpolation with defaults and required values
body:
Compose supports shell-style variable expansion with defaults and error messages.

```yaml
services:
  app:
    image: myapp:${TAG:-latest}           # Default: latest
    ports:
      - "${PORT:-3000}:3000"              # Default: 3000
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=${DATABASE_URL:?DATABASE_URL is required}  # Error if missing
      - LOG_LEVEL=${LOG_LEVEL:-info}       # Default: info
      - NODE_ENV=${NODE_ENV}               # Empty string if not set
```

```bash
# .env file (auto-loaded by Compose)
TAG=1.2.3
PORT=8080
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://localhost:5432/app

# Override at runtime
TAG=canary docker compose up

# Validate required vars
docker compose config  # Shows interpolated config, errors on missing required vars
```

The `${VAR:?error}` syntax stops Compose startup with your error message if the variable is unset or empty. Use `docker compose config` to preview the fully interpolated file.

title: Docker daemon.json production configuration
Docker .dockerignore for Node.js projects — minimal build context
body:
A comprehensive `.dockerignore` that keeps the build context small and secure.

```
# Version control
.git
.gitignore

# Dependencies (will be installed in container)
node_modules

# Build output (will be built in container)
dist
build
.next

# Development files
.env
.env.*
!.env.example
*.md
LICENSE

# IDE
.vscode
.idea
*.swp
*.swo

# Testing
coverage
.nyc_output
*.test.ts
*.spec.ts
__tests__

# Docker files (don't need to be in context)
Dockerfile*
docker-compose*
compose.yaml
compose.override.yaml
.dockerignore

# CI/CD
.github
.gitlab-ci.yml

# OS files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
```

Test your `.dockerignore` by checking context size: build with `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=0` temporarily to see `Sending build context to Docker daemon` message. A well-configured ignore file typically reduces context from 500MB+ to under 5MB.

title: Docker Compose production config with restart policies and logging
body:
Production compose configuration with proper restart policies, resource limits, and log management.

```yaml
services:
  app:
    image: ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:${TAG}
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    environment:
      NODE_ENV: production
      DATABASE_URL: ${DATABASE_URL:?Required}
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: '2.0'
          memory: 512M
        reservations:
          cpus: '0.5'
          memory: 256M
    logging:
      driver: json-file
      options:
        max-size: "10m"
        max-file: "5"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "node", "-e", "fetch('http://localhost:3000/health').then(r => process.exit(r.ok ? 0 : 1))"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 30s

  db:
    image: postgres:16
    restart: unless-stopped
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD_FILE: /run/secrets/db_password
    secrets:
      - db_password
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 1G
    shm_size: 256m  # Shared memory for Postgres

secrets:
  db_password:
    file: ./secrets/db_password.txt

volumes:
  pgdata:
```

Use `restart: unless-stopped` (not `always`) so you can intentionally stop services. Set `shm_size` for Postgres to avoid "could not resize shared memory segment" errors.

title: Docker BuildKit Dockerfile with mount cache and bind mounts
body:
Advanced Dockerfile using BuildKit features for maximum build performance.

```dockerfile
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1

FROM node:20-slim AS base
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production

# Install system dependencies with apt cache mount
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/var/cache/apt \
    --mount=type=cache,target=/var/lib/apt/lists \
    apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    openssl ca-certificates

# Install Node dependencies with npm cache mount
FROM base AS deps
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.npm \
    npm ci --omit=dev

# Build stage
FROM base AS build
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.npm \
    npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Final stage — minimal runtime image
FROM gcr.io/distroless/nodejs20-debian12
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
COPY package.json ./
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["dist/server.js"]
```

The `# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1` directive enables BuildKit frontend features. Cache mounts persist across builds on the same builder but never appear in the final image. Using distroless as the final base gives you the smallest possible image with no shell or system utilities.

title: Docker Compose networking config with custom subnets and aliases
body:
Define custom networks with specific subnets for predictable IP ranges, and use aliases for service discovery.

```yaml
services:
  api:
    build: ./api
    networks:
      backend:
        aliases:
          - api.local  # Additional DNS name
        ipv4_address: 172.20.0.10  # Static IP

  web:
    build: ./web
    networks:
      frontend:
      backend:
        aliases:
          - web.internal

  nginx:
    image: nginx
    ports: ["80:80", "443:443"]
    networks:
      frontend:
        ipv4_address: 172.21.0.10

networks:
  frontend:
    driver: bridge
    ipam:
      config:
        - subnet: 172.21.0.0/24
          gateway: 172.21.0.1
  backend:
    driver: bridge
    internal: true  # No external internet access
    ipam:
      config:
        - subnet: 172.20.0.0/24
```

The `internal: true` flag prevents containers on the backend network from reaching the internet — useful for database containers that should only be accessible internally. Aliases provide additional DNS names beyond the service name.

title: Docker Compose with Traefik reverse proxy and automatic SSL
body:
Traefik auto-discovers services via Docker labels and provisions Let's Encrypt certificates.

```yaml
services:
  traefik:
    image: traefik:v3.0
    command:
      - --providers.docker=true
      - --providers.docker.exposedbydefault=false
      - --entrypoints.web.address=:80
      - --entrypoints.websecure.address=:443
      - --certificatesresolvers.letsencrypt.acme.email=admin@example.com
      - --certificatesresolvers.letsencrypt.acme.storage=/certs/acme.json
      - --certificatesresolvers.letsencrypt.acme.httpchallenge.entrypoint=web
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
    volumes:
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro
      - certs:/certs

  api:
    build: ./api
    labels:
      - traefik.enable=true
      - traefik.http.routers.api.rule=Host(`api.example.com`)
      - traefik.http.routers.api.entrypoints=websecure
      - traefik.http.routers.api.tls.certresolver=letsencrypt
      - traefik.http.services.api.loadbalancer.server.port=3000

  web:
    build: ./web
    labels:
      - traefik.enable=true
      - traefik.http.routers.web.rule=Host(`example.com`)
      - traefik.http.routers.web.entrypoints=websecure
      - traefik.http.routers.web.tls.certresolver=letsencrypt

volumes:
  certs:
```

Traefik reads Docker labels to configure routing. `exposedbydefault=false` means services must opt in with `traefik.enable=true`. Mount the Docker socket as read-only for security.

title: Docker Compose GPU passthrough for ML/AI workloads
body:
Pass NVIDIA GPUs to containers for machine learning training and inference.

```yaml
services:
  ml-training:
    image: pytorch/pytorch:2.2.0-cuda12.1-cudnn8-runtime
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: all  # Or specific: count: 1
              capabilities: [gpu]
    volumes:
      - ./models:/workspace/models
      - ./data:/workspace/data
    environment:
      - NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=all
    shm_size: '8g'  # Shared memory for DataLoader workers

  inference:
    build: ./inference
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              device_ids: ['0']  # Specific GPU
              capabilities: [gpu]
```

Prerequisites: NVIDIA Container Toolkit must be installed on the host. Test with: `docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.1-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi`. The `shm_size` is critical for PyTorch DataLoader with `num_workers > 0`.

title: Docker multi-platform build config with platform-specific stages
body:
When you need different build steps for different architectures, use platform-specific stages.

```dockerfile
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1

FROM --platform=$BUILDPLATFORM node:20-slim AS builder
ARG TARGETPLATFORM
ARG BUILDPLATFORM
RUN echo "Building on $BUILDPLATFORM for $TARGETPLATFORM"
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Platform-specific binary download
FROM builder AS binary-amd64
RUN curl -L https://example.com/tool-linux-amd64 -o /usr/local/bin/tool

FROM builder AS binary-arm64
RUN curl -L https://example.com/tool-linux-arm64 -o /usr/local/bin/tool

# Select the right binary based on target
FROM binary-${TARGETARCH} AS binary

# Final image
FROM node:20-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=binary /usr/local/bin/tool /usr/local/bin/tool
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

```bash
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
  -t myapp:latest --push .
```

`$BUILDPLATFORM` is the platform running the build. `$TARGETPLATFORM` is the target. Using `--platform=$BUILDPLATFORM` on the builder stage runs it natively (fast) even for cross-platform builds.

title: Docker Compose development database with persistent data and init scripts
body:
Set up development databases with initialization scripts, custom configuration, and persistent data.

```yaml
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16
    ports: ["5432:5432"]
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp_dev
      POSTGRES_USER: dev
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: devpass
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./docker/postgres/init:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d  # Run on first start
      - ./docker/postgres/postgresql.conf:/etc/postgresql/postgresql.conf
    command: postgres -c config_file=/etc/postgresql/postgresql.conf
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U dev -d myapp_dev"]
      interval: 5s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports: ["6379:6379"]
    command: redis-server --maxmemory 128mb --maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
    volumes:
      - redisdata:/data

volumes:
  pgdata:
  redisdata:
```

```sql
-- docker/postgres/init/01-extensions.sql
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS "uuid-ossp";
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS "pgcrypto";
```

Init scripts in `/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/` only run when the data volume is empty (first start). To re-run, delete the volume: `docker compose down -v`.

title: Docker tmpfs mount for ephemeral data and test databases
body:
Use tmpfs mounts to store data in memory for speed. Ideal for test databases and temporary files.

```yaml
services:
  # Fast test database — data in RAM
  test-db:
    image: postgres:16
    tmpfs:
      - /var/lib/postgresql/data  # DB in memory
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: test
      POSTGRES_USER: test
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
    # Postgres tuning for speed over durability
    command: >
      postgres
      -c fsync=off
      -c synchronous_commit=off
      -c full_page_writes=off

  app-test:
    build:
      context: .
      target: test
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp:size=100M  # Fast temp storage with size limit
    depends_on:
      test-db:
        condition: service_healthy
```

```bash
# docker run with tmpfs
docker run --tmpfs /tmp:rw,noexec,nosuid,size=100m myapp
```

Data in tmpfs is lost when the container stops. This is perfect for CI test databases — each test run gets a clean, fast database without disk I/O overhead.

title: Docker security scanning in CI with Trivy and fail thresholds
body:
Integrate container security scanning into your CI pipeline with configurable severity thresholds.

```yaml
# .github/workflows/security.yml
name: Security Scan
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build image
        run: docker build -t myapp:${{ github.sha }} .

      - name: Run Trivy vulnerability scanner
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
        with:
          image-ref: myapp:${{ github.sha }}
          format: 'sarif'
          output: 'trivy-results.sarif'
          severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH'
          exit-code: '1'  # Fail pipeline on findings

      - name: Upload scan results
        uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3
        if: always()
        with:
          sarif_file: 'trivy-results.sarif'
```

```bash
# Local scanning
trivy image myapp:latest
trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 myapp:latest

# Scan Dockerfile for misconfigurations
trivy config .

# Scan filesystem (find secrets, misconfigs)
trivy fs --security-checks secret,config .
```

Trivy detects OS package vulnerabilities, language-specific dependency vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and embedded secrets.

title: Docker entrypoint script with wait-for-it and config templating
body:
A robust entrypoint script that waits for dependencies and renders configuration from environment variables.

```bash
#!/bin/sh
set -e

# Wait for database to be ready
wait_for_service() {
  local host=$1 port=$2 timeout=${3:-30}
  echo "Waiting for $host:$port..."
  for i in $(seq 1 $timeout); do
    if nc -z "$host" "$port" 2>/dev/null; then
      echo "$host:$port is available"
      return 0
    fi
    sleep 1
  done
  echo "Timeout waiting for $host:$port"
  return 1
}

# Parse DATABASE_URL for host and port
DB_HOST=$(echo "$DATABASE_URL" | sed -n 's|.*@\([^:]*\):.*|\1|p')
DB_PORT=$(echo "$DATABASE_URL" | sed -n 's|.*:\([0-9]*\)/.*|\1|p')

wait_for_service "$DB_HOST" "$DB_PORT"

# Run migrations if requested
if [ "$RUN_MIGRATIONS" = "true" ]; then
  echo "Running database migrations..."
  npx prisma migrate deploy
fi

# Template config from env vars
envsubst < /app/config.template.json > /app/config.json

# Use exec to replace shell with app (proper PID 1 signal handling)
exec "$@"
```

```dockerfile
RUN apk add --no-cache gettext  # For envsubst
COPY entrypoint.sh /entrypoint.sh
RUN chmod +x /entrypoint.sh
ENTRYPOINT ["/entrypoint.sh"]
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

The `exec "$@"` at the end replaces the shell with whatever CMD is passed, so your app becomes PID 1 and receives signals correctly.

title: Docker cleanup script for development machines
body:
A comprehensive cleanup script to reclaim disk space from Docker without destroying active containers.

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -e

echo "=== Docker Disk Usage ==="
docker system df

echo ""
echo "=== Cleaning up ==="

# Remove stopped containers
STOPPED=$(docker ps -aq --filter status=exited | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
if [ "$STOPPED" -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "Removing $STOPPED stopped containers..."
  docker container prune -f
fi

# Remove dangling images (untagged)
DANGLING=$(docker images -qf dangling=true | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
if [ "$DANGLING" -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "Removing $DANGLING dangling images..."
  docker image prune -f
fi

# Remove images older than 7 days (not used by running containers)
echo "Removing images older than 7 days..."
docker image prune -a --filter "until=168h" -f

# Remove unused volumes
echo "Removing unused volumes..."
docker volume prune -f

# Remove unused networks
echo "Removing unused networks..."
docker network prune -f

# Remove build cache older than 7 days
echo "Removing old build cache..."
docker builder prune --filter "until=168h" -f

echo ""
echo "=== After Cleanup ==="
docker system df
```

Add to crontab for automatic weekly cleanup: `0 2 * * 0 /path/to/docker-cleanup.sh >> /var/log/docker-cleanup.log 2>&1`.

title: Docker container debugging commands cheat sheet
body:
Essential commands for debugging running and exited containers.

```bash
# View logs (follow, with timestamps)
docker logs -f --tail 100 --timestamps container_name

# Exec into running container
docker exec -it container_name /bin/sh
docker exec -it container_name /bin/bash

# Run a debug container in the same network namespace
docker run -it --rm --network container:container_name \
  nicolaka/netshoot  # Includes curl, dig, tcpdump, etc.

# Inspect container details
docker inspect container_name | jq '.[0].State'
docker inspect container_name | jq '.[0].NetworkSettings.Networks'
docker inspect container_name | jq '.[0].Config.Env'

# Check resource usage
docker stats container_name --no-stream

# Copy files from container
docker cp container_name:/app/logs/error.log ./error.log

# View filesystem changes since container started
docker diff container_name

# Attach to container's stdout/stderr
docker attach --sig-proxy=false container_name

# Debug an exited container — start it with a shell
docker commit exited_container debug_image
docker run -it debug_image /bin/sh
```

The `nicolaka/netshoot` container is invaluable for network debugging — it includes every network tool you'd need and runs in the same network namespace as the target container.

title: Docker image size analysis and optimization
body:
Tools and techniques to analyze and reduce Docker image size.

```bash
# View layer sizes
docker history myapp:latest --no-trunc

# Use dive for interactive layer exploration
# Install: https://github.com/wagoodman/dive
dive myapp:latest

# Compare image sizes
docker images --format '{{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}} {{.Size}}' | sort -k2 -h

# Export and analyze image contents
docker save myapp:latest | tar -tv | sort -k3 -rn | head -20
```

```dockerfile
# Size optimization techniques

# 1. Use multi-stage builds (biggest impact)
FROM node:20 AS builder
RUN npm ci && npm run build
FROM node:20-slim
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist

# 2. Combine and clean in same layer
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends curl && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# 3. Use .dockerignore (reduces build context + accidental COPY)

# 4. Pin slim/alpine base images
FROM python:3.12-slim  # ~150MB vs python:3.12 ~1GB

# 5. Copy only what's needed
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
# NOT: COPY --from=builder /app .
```

A typical Node.js app: `node:20` base is 1.1GB, `node:20-slim` is 200MB, multi-stage with slim is ~150MB, with distroless is ~120MB.

title: Docker Compose wait-for-healthy script for integration tests
body:
A script to start services, wait for all health checks, run tests, then tear down.

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -e

COMPOSE_FILE="compose.test.yaml"

cleanup() {
  echo "Tearing down test environment..."
  docker compose -f $COMPOSE_FILE down -v --remove-orphans
}
trap cleanup EXIT

# Start services in background
echo "Starting test services..."
docker compose -f $COMPOSE_FILE up -d

# Wait for all services to be healthy
echo "Waiting for services to be healthy..."
TIMEOUT=60
START=$(date +%s)

while true; do
  UNHEALTHY=$(docker compose -f $COMPOSE_FILE ps --format json | \
    jq -r 'select(.Health != "healthy" and .Health != "") | .Name' | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
  
  if [ "$UNHEALTHY" -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "All services healthy!"
    break
  fi

  ELAPSED=$(( $(date +%s) - START ))
  if [ "$ELAPSED" -gt "$TIMEOUT" ]; then
    echo "Timeout waiting for services"
    docker compose -f $COMPOSE_FILE ps
    docker compose -f $COMPOSE_FILE logs
    exit 1
  fi

  sleep 2
done

# Run tests
echo "Running integration tests..."
docker compose -f $COMPOSE_FILE exec -T app npm run test:integration
TEST_EXIT=$?

exit $TEST_EXIT
```

The `trap cleanup EXIT` ensures containers are always torn down, even if the script fails or is interrupted.

title: Docker Alpine vs Slim vs Distroless base images comparison
body:
Choosing the right base image involves tradeoffs between size, compatibility, and debuggability.

| Image | Size (Node 20) | Shell | Package Manager | glibc | Best For |
|-------|----------------|-------|-----------------|-------|----------|
| `node:20` | ~1.1GB | Yes | apt | Yes | Development |
| `node:20-slim` | ~200MB | Yes | apt | Yes | Production (general) |
| `node:20-alpine` | ~180MB | Yes | apk | No (musl) | Production (if no native deps) |
| `gcr.io/distroless/nodejs20` | ~120MB | No | None | Yes | Production (hardened) |

**Alpine**: Smallest with shell access, but musl libc causes compatibility issues with native Node addons (bcrypt, sharp, prisma). Requires `python3 make g++` for compilation.

**Slim (Debian)**: Best balance of size and compatibility. glibc means precompiled binaries work. Has shell for debugging.

**Distroless**: Smallest attack surface, no shell, no package manager. Can't exec into the container for debugging. Use `:debug` variant for troubleshooting.

Recommendation: Use `slim` unless you have specific reasons to use Alpine (no native deps) or distroless (strict security requirements).

title: Docker Compose v1 vs v2 — key migration differences
body:
Docker Compose v2 is a complete rewrite as a Docker CLI plugin. Key differences that affect existing workflows:

| Feature | v1 (`docker-compose`) | v2 (`docker compose`) |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Command | `docker-compose` | `docker compose` (no hyphen) |
| Container naming | `project_service_1` | `project-service-1` (dashes) |
| Version field | Required | Ignored (warning) |
| `depends_on` conditions | Limited | `service_healthy`, `service_completed_successfully` |
| `extends` | Supported | Re-introduced (was missing in v3) |
| `profiles` | Not available | Supported |
| `develop.watch` | Not available | Supported |
| Resource limits | Only with Swarm | Works without Swarm |
| Build | Legacy builder | BuildKit by default |

**Migration steps:**
1. Remove `version` field from compose files
2. Replace `docker-compose` with `docker compose` in scripts
3. Update any code that references container names (underscore → dash)
4. Move `deploy.resources` — they now work without Swarm mode
5. Test `depends_on` behavior — v2 is stricter about health checks

Compose v1 reached end-of-life in July 2023. All new projects should use v2.

title: Docker bind mounts vs named volumes vs tmpfs — when to use each
body:
Docker offers three mount types, each with different use cases and performance characteristics.

| Type | Persistence | Performance | Use Case |
|------|------------|-------------|----------|
| Bind mount | Host filesystem | Native (Linux), slower on Mac | Development, config files |
| Named volume | Docker-managed | Consistent, optimized | Database data, persistent state |
| tmpfs | RAM only | Fastest | Temp files, test databases, secrets |

```yaml
services:
  app:
    volumes:
      # Bind mount — host path:container path
      - ./src:/app/src

      # Named volume — managed by Docker
      - node_modules:/app/node_modules

      # tmpfs — in-memory
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp

volumes:
  node_modules:  # Named volume declaration
```

**Key differences:**
- Bind mounts: host directory overlays container directory completely. Changes are bidirectional. On macOS, I/O is slower due to filesystem translation.
- Named volumes: Docker initializes the volume with the container directory's contents on first use. Better performance on Mac.
- tmpfs: Data lost on container stop. No disk I/O. Great for sensitive data that shouldn't persist.

For macOS performance, consider `consistency: cached` or switch to named volumes for heavy I/O directories like `node_modules`.

title: Docker networking drivers — bridge vs host vs none vs overlay
body:
Docker provides multiple network drivers for different isolation and performance needs.

| Driver | Isolation | Performance | Multi-host | Use Case |
|--------|-----------|-------------|------------|----------|
| bridge | Container-level | Good | No | Default, most containers |
| host | None (shares host) | Best | No | Performance-critical, >10k connections |
| none | Complete | N/A | No | Security-sensitive, batch jobs |
| overlay | Container-level | Good | Yes | Swarm/multi-host |
| macvlan | VLAN-level | Best | Yes | Legacy apps needing direct LAN access |

```yaml
# Bridge (default) — containers get private IPs
services:
  app:
    networks: [mynet]

# Host — container uses host's network stack directly
services:
  perf-app:
    network_mode: host
    # No port mapping needed — app listens on host ports directly

# None — no networking
services:
  batch:
    network_mode: none

# Container — share another container's network
services:
  sidecar:
    network_mode: service:app  # Same network namespace as app
```

`host` networking eliminates NAT overhead but sacrifices isolation. Use it for high-throughput services where port mapping overhead matters. `service:` mode is used for sidecar patterns (e.g., Envoy proxy, log collectors).

title: Docker vs Podman — key differences for container workflows
body:
Podman is a drop-in Docker replacement that runs daemonless and rootless by default.

| Feature | Docker | Podman |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Architecture | Client-server (daemon) | Daemonless (fork/exec) |
| Root required | Yes (daemon), optional (rootless) | No (rootless by default) |
| Compose | `docker compose` | `podman-compose` or `podman compose` |
| Pods | No (only in Swarm) | Native pod support |
| Systemd integration | Requires config | Native (quadlet) |
| Docker socket | Required | Optional compatibility |
| Build tool | BuildKit | Buildah |
| Registry | Docker Hub default | No default (user chooses) |

```bash
# Podman is mostly command-compatible
alias docker=podman

# Generate systemd unit files from containers
podman generate systemd --new --name myapp

# Generate Kubernetes YAML from running containers
podman generate kube myapp > myapp.yaml

# Run pods (like Kubernetes pods)
podman pod create --name mypod -p 8080:80
podman run --pod mypod nginx
podman run --pod mypod myapp
```

Podman's daemonless architecture means containers survive `podman` process crashes. Docker's daemon model means all containers die if the daemon crashes (mitigated by `live-restore`). For development, Docker Desktop has a better experience. For production on Linux, Podman is increasingly preferred.
